title: Gloomhaven
resolution_core: simultaneous two-card selection; each card split into top/bottom actions; attacks draw from a personal modifier deck

vector_explanations.actions.selection.action_cards_hand: Each turn every player secretly selects two ability cards from their class hand; one provides the top action, the other the bottom action, defining everything the character does that round.
vector_explanations.turns.order.card_determined_initiative: The initiative number printed on one of your two chosen cards sets your place in the round order, interleaved with monster ability cards drawn from each monster type's deck.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.planning_then_execution_spl: All players pick their two cards simultaneously without revealing numbers, then the round resolves in strict initiative order, so planning and execution are cleanly separated phases.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.modifier_deck: Every attack draws from the attacker's modifier deck of +0/+1/-1/x2/null cards; perks remove or add cards, and Curse and Bless shuffle temporary null or x2 cards into it.
vector_explanations.combat.positioning.hex_grid: Scenarios play on hex-tile map boards; movement, attack range, area-of-effect shapes, and summon placement are all counted in hexes.
vector_explanations.combat.positioning.line_of_sight: Ranged attacks require line of sight traced corner-to-corner between hexes, blocked by walls, so positioning around door tiles and obstacles matters constantly.
vector_explanations.combat.status.conditions_tracking: Tokens track Poison, Wound, Immobilize, Disarm, Stun, Muddle, Strengthen, and Invisible on monster stat sleeves and player boards, each with codified expiry timing.
vector_explanations.actions.points.exhaustion_limits: A character who cannot play or rest with two cards in hand, or who drops to zero HP, becomes exhausted and is removed for the scenario, making card stamina a hard action budget.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Cards go to discard or are permanently lost when played for strong effects; managing your shrinking hand against scenario length is the game's central tension.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.multi_use_cards: Any card can substitute a default Attack 2 as a top action or Move 2 as a bottom action, so every card is always at least two other things besides its printed abilities.
vector_explanations.combat.restoration.short_rest: At the end of a round you may short rest, shuffling your discard back to hand but losing one random card (redraw once by taking a wound).
vector_explanations.combat.restoration.long_rest: A long rest costs an entire turn at initiative 99 but lets you choose the card lost, heal 2 HP, and refresh spent items, trading tempo for control.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: One to four mercenaries fight scenarios together against a fully systematized monster AI; there is no traitor and no player-versus-player conflict.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.boss_ai_decks: Each monster type acts via its own shuffled ability-card deck, which sets initiative and behavior per round; bosses have unique decks and stat cards with special abilities.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.scaling_difficulty: Scenario level is set from average party level, scaling monster level, trap damage, and gold conversion; the rules offer easy and hard adjustments of plus or minus one level.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.legacy_envelopes: Sealed envelopes and boxes (including new playable classes) are opened only when specific personal quests or global achievements are completed.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.sticker_permanen: Achievement stickers, scenario-completion stickers, and enhancement stickers are applied permanently to the map board, party sheet, and ability cards.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.branching_scenar: Completed scenarios unlock linked scenarios on a branching tree of 95 numbered entries, and story choices can permanently lock out alternative branches.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.campaign_log_she: A party sheet records reputation, achievements, and unlocked scenarios, while each character sheet logs gold, XP, perk ticks, and personal quest progress between sessions.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.persistent_upgra: Purchased items, ability-card enhancements bought with gold, and city prosperity all persist across scenarios for the life of the campaign.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.character_retiremen: Each character has a secret personal quest; completing it forces retirement, unlocking a new class envelope and granting the town a prosperity boost and a new city event.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: XP is earned from printed values on played ability cards and scenario completion; hitting thresholds raises level, adding a perk and a higher-level card to your pool.
vector_explanations.character.progression.perks: Perk checkmarks, earned from leveling and battle goals, buy class-specific modifier-deck edits such as removing -1 cards or adding rolling Fire +1 cards.
vector_explanations.character.progression.prosperity_level: Gloomhaven's prosperity track rises through events and donations, unlocking higher starting levels for new characters and expanding the item shop's stock.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.class_based: Characters are one of 17 asymmetric classes (6 starting, 11 locked), each with a unique ability-card pool, hand size, perk list, and HP progression.
vector_explanations.equipment.economy_of_gear.equip_slots: Items occupy strict slots: one head, one body, one legs, up to two one-handed or one two-handed weapon, and a number of small items equal to half your level rounded up.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.shop_items: Between scenarios characters buy and sell items in Gloomhaven's shop at fixed prices; available item designs expand as prosperity rises and new designs are looted.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.legacy_map_stickers: Unlocked scenario locations are marked with numbered stickers on the fold-out world map, permanently recording the party's route through the campaign.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.road_events: Traveling to a non-linked scenario forces a road event card (city events when returning): a read-aloud choice with A/B outcomes that can add curses, gold, or new cards to the event decks.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Each scenario defines an explicit objective such as kill all revealed enemies, destroy an altar, or survive a set number of rounds; meeting it wins regardless of losses.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.hidden_objectives: Each scenario deals every player a secret battle goal card; fulfilling it (e.g., take no damage, kill three or more enemies) earns perk checkmarks at scenario end.
vector_explanations.exploration.discovery.unlocking_content: Envelopes, new classes, scenarios, items, and event cards are gated behind achievements, retirements, and donations, steadily expanding the component pool over the campaign.

title: Frosthaven
resolution_core: simultaneous two-card selection with top/bottom actions and attack modifier decks, plus a between-scenario outpost phase

vector_explanations.actions.selection.action_cards_hand: As in Gloomhaven, players simultaneously choose two ability cards each round; the leading card's number sets initiative while top and bottom halves define the turn's actions.
vector_explanations.turns.order.card_determined_initiative: Initiative comes from the printed number on your designated leading card, slotted into a round order alongside monster ability cards drawn per monster type.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.modifier_deck: Attacks flip cards from a personal attack modifier deck; masteries and perks sculpt it, and several Frosthaven classes manipulate their own decks (e.g., the Blinkblade's time tokens interacting with card play).
vector_explanations.combat.positioning.hex_grid: Combat plays out on hex map tiles with range, movement, push/pull, and area templates all measured in hexes, including new overlay terrain like ice that causes slides.
vector_explanations.combat.status.conditions_tracking: Poison, Wound, Brittle, Bane, Impair, Stun, and other conditions are tracked with tokens; Frosthaven adds new conditions such as Brittle (double damage) and Bane (10 damage then removal).
vector_explanations.actions.points.exhaustion_limits: Characters exhaust when unable to rest or play two cards, or at zero HP; because lost cards rarely return, every big play permanently shortens the scenario clock.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.multi_use_cards: Every ability card can be played as a default Attack 2 on top or Move 2 on bottom, so hands never contain dead cards, only opportunity costs.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Discarded cards can be recovered by resting at the cost of losing one card permanently for the scenario, making hand attrition the real hit-point track.
vector_explanations.combat.restoration.long_rest: A long rest takes your whole turn at initiative 99, heals 2, refreshes spent items, and lets you choose which card to lose, versus the random loss of a short rest.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: Up to four players cooperate against automated monster decks across a 100+ scenario campaign; there is no hidden traitor or competitive scoring.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.boss_ai_decks: Monster types each run on shuffled ability decks that determine initiative and behavior; bosses and named characters get bespoke decks and stat cards.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.scaling_difficulty: Scenario level derives from average party level with optional adjustments; monster stat cards carry seven level rows so difficulty scales continuously through the campaign.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.class_based: Six new starting classes (Drifter, Blinkblade, Banner Spear, Deathwalker, Boneshaper, Geminate) plus ten unlockable classes each bring unique card pools and mechanics.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: XP printed on played cards and scenario rewards drive levels 1-9, each granting a new card choice, a perk mark, and HP; masteries add extra perk marks for class challenges.
vector_explanations.character.progression.perks: Perk sheets now include class masteries; checkmarks purchase modifier-deck edits such as replacing -1 cards or adding element-infusing rolling modifiers.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.character_retiremen: Personal quest cards force retirement on completion, unlocking envelopes or buildings and adding the retiree to the town's roster of citizens for future events.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.branching_scenar: The scenario flowchart branches on story choices and outcomes; some chains lock out others, and seasonal sections gate scenarios behind campaign progress.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.campaign_log_she: A campaign sheet tracks morale, soldiers, resources, buildings, and calendar weeks, while character sheets log XP, resources, masteries, and perk ticks.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.sticker_permanen: Building stickers upgrade the town map permanently, scenario stickers mark the region map, and enhancement stickers permanently modify ability cards.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.legacy_envelopes: Sealed envelopes, boxes, and the mysterious locked chest open on specific triggers, revealing classes, buildings, and story content that permanently changes the campaign.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.persistent_upgra: Crafted items, built and upgraded buildings, ability enhancements, and character masteries all persist week to week across the campaign calendar.
vector_explanations.exploration.discovery.unlocking_content: New classes, buildings, items, scenarios, and even rule systems (like enhancements, which must be unlocked by building the Enhancer) are gated behind campaign play.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Every scenario states an explicit objective - kill all enemies, escort NPCs, destroy objects, or survive - and success is judged solely against that condition.
vector_explanations.simulation.building.settlement_managemen: The outpost phase runs the town of Frosthaven: passive income, building effects, upgrades, and morale are resolved on a dedicated town map between scenarios.
vector_explanations.campaign.board_campaign.shared_settlemen: Players collectively spend lumber, metal, and hide to construct and level buildings (Craftsman, Alchemist, Barracks, walls), whose benefits are shared by all characters.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.seasonal_cycle_structure: A campaign calendar advances one week per scenario; summer and winter alternate every ten weeks, changing available scenarios, events, and outpost dangers.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.seasons_events: Separate summer and winter outpost event decks fire each outpost phase, with winter events skewing harsher; attacks on the town damage buildings and morale.
vector_explanations.equipment.crafting.recipe_crafting: Items are no longer simply bought: the Craftsman building converts specific resource combinations listed on item cards into gear, and the Alchemist brews potions from herbs.
vector_explanations.equipment.crafting.material_harvesting: Lumber, metal, and hide come from loot cards and scenario rewards, while six herb types feed potion brewing; resource icons on the loot deck make harvesting a mid-scenario decision.
vector_explanations.equipment.economy_of_gear.loot_decks: Looting draws from a scenario-built loot deck of money, resources, herbs, and random items; the deck is seeded per scenario, and 1418-series cards add campaign flavor.
vector_explanations.downtime.projects.base_improvements: Building projects consume resources and calendar time across outpost phases, from palisade walls that mitigate winter attacks to the Enhancer, Mining Camp, and Logging Camp.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.legacy_map_stickers: Discovered scenario locations are stickered onto the wilderness map around Frosthaven, permanently charting the party's exploration of the northern frontier.

title: Twilight Imperium: Fourth Edition
resolution_core: strategy card selection with follow actions; combat rolls d10s per unit vs printed combat values; first to 10 VP wins

vector_explanations.actions.selection.role_selection: Each round opens with players drafting one of eight numbered strategy cards (Leadership, Diplomacy, Politics, Construction, Trade, Warfare, Technology, Imperial), each granting a unique primary ability.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.follow_actions: When a player pops a strategy card's primary ability, every other player may follow by spending a strategy-pool command token to use its secondary ability, so every activation ripples around the table.
vector_explanations.turns.order.card_determined_initiative: Initiative order for the action and status phases is set by the lowest strategy-card number held, making the Leadership card (1) a turn-order weapon.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Rounds run Strategy phase (draft cards), Action phase (alternating single actions), Status phase (score, repair, redraw), and after Mecatol Rex is claimed, the Agenda phase of galactic politics.
vector_explanations.turns.variance.pass_and_stay_out: During the action phase players alternate taking exactly one action; a player who has exhausted their strategy card may pass and takes no further turns that round.
vector_explanations.actions.points.action_point_allowance: Command tokens allocated among tactic, fleet, and strategy pools are the game's action currency: tactical activations, follows, and fleet capacity all spend from these finite pools.
vector_explanations.politics.governance.council_votes: Once the custodians token is claimed, each Agenda phase reveals two laws or directives that all players vote on, spending the influence values of exhausted planets as votes; passed laws permanently change rules.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.asymmetrical_abilities: Seventeen factions have unique abilities, starting fleets, home systems, flagship stats, faction technologies, and promissory notes, from the Arborec's Mitosis to the Jol-Nar's tech discounts.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.dudes_on_a_map: Plastic fleets, ground forces, PDS, and space docks occupy hex systems; presence and control of planets drive objectives, votes, and production.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.modular_boards: The galaxy is assembled from hexagonal system tiles around Mecatol Rex before play, with prescribed or drafted layouts making every map different.
vector_explanations.logistics.movement.wormhole_tiles: Alpha and beta wormhole tiles are adjacent to their twins across the map, enabling long jumps; the Ghosts of Creuss faction and the wormhole nexus extend this system further.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Ships move between adjacent hex systems up to their printed move value during tactical activations, with anomalies (asteroid fields, supernovas, nebulae, gravity rifts) restricting paths.
vector_explanations.combat.space.battle_resolution: Space combat proceeds in rounds: anti-fighter barrage, then each ship rolls a d10 hitting on its combat value or better; hits assign to the defender's ships until one side is destroyed or retreats.
vector_explanations.combat.space.fleet_formations: Fleet pool tokens cap the number of non-fighter ships allowed in any one system, and capital ships carry capacity limits for fighters and ground forces.
vector_explanations.combat.ranged.siege_phase: Invasions run a bombardment step (rolling against planets, blocked by planetary shield), space cannon fire from PDS units, then ground combat between committed ground forces.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: All combat, bombardment, space cannon, and anti-fighter barrage use pools of d10s where each die meeting the unit's combat value scores one hit.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resource_generation: Planet cards are exhausted to spend their printed resource values on production and their influence values on votes and command tokens, refreshing each status phase.
vector_explanations.social.negotiation_bg.trade_negotiation: The Trade strategy card refreshes commodities, which convert to tradeable trade goods when given to other players, fueling a constant table economy of goods-for-favors deals.
vector_explanations.social.negotiation_bg.non_binding_pacts: The rules explicitly distinguish binding transactions (exchanging goods, promissory notes) from non-binding deals: promises about future actions may be freely broken.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_limits: Players hold action cards gained from Politics and status phases with a hard hand limit of seven, forcing discards of sabotages, rider bets, and combat tricks.
vector_explanations.simulation.technology.research_trees: Technologies in four colored trees require prerequisite icons of matching colors; unit-upgrade techs (Dreadnought II, Fighter II) sit atop these chains and transform fleet capabilities.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.unit_recruitment: Space docks give Production values that build ships and ground forces in the activated system, paying resources per unit up to the dock's production limit.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.finite_supply: Reinforcements are strictly component-limited: you cannot build more units than the plastic in your supply, and command token counts constrain how much you can act each round.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.public_milestones: Stage I and Stage II public objective cards are revealed one per round; each may be claimed once per player during the status phase for one or two victory points.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.hidden_objectives: Players draw secret objectives (e.g., win a combat in a home system) and may score up to three across the game, revealing them for a VP each when their condition is met.
vector_explanations.character.progression.victory_points: All scoring feeds a shared victory point track from 0 to 10, with the Imperial strategy card letting Mecatol Rex's holder mint a point per round.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.race_to_finish: The game ends immediately when a player reaches 10 victory points (or 14 in the long variant), making the final rounds a tense race of objective timing.

title: Root
resolution_core: asymmetric faction turns; battles roll two 0-3 dice, attacker takes the higher; first to 30 VP or dominance wins

vector_explanations.politics.factions.asymmetrical_abilities: The four base factions play almost entirely different games: the Marquise builds an economy, the Eyrie programs a decree, the Alliance foments revolt, and the Vagabond adventures solo.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.animal_species_asymmet: Each woodland species is a faction with its own board, rules panel, and win pacing - industrial cats, imperial birds, insurgent mice-foxes-rabbits, and a wandering raccoon.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.eyrie_decree: The Eyrie must add cards to its Decree columns (Recruit, Move, Battle, Build) every turn and fully execute all of them; failure triggers Turmoil, discarding the decree and losing points.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.marquise_industrial_en: The Marquise de Cat places wood from sawmills to build workshops, sawmills, and recruiters, scoring escalating points per building type as her engine spreads from the Keep.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.woodland_alliance_symp: The Alliance spreads sympathy tokens that tax enemy movement (Outrage grants cards), and spends matching supporter cards to spark revolts that wipe a clearing and place a base.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.vagabond_quest: The Vagabond completes quest cards matching a clearing's suit by exhausting listed items, and shifts relationships with factions by aiding them or turning Hostile through battle.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.coalition_loyalty: A losing Vagabond may play a dominance card to form a coalition with the lowest-scoring player, winning jointly if that partner wins, a formalized kingmaking valve.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.asymmetric_action_menu: Each faction's turn follows its own Birdsong/Daylight/Evening script printed on its board, with completely different action menus and per-phase allowances.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.dudes_on_a_map: Warriors, buildings, and tokens occupy the twelve forest clearings; military presence enables battles, rule, crafting bonuses, and faction-specific scoring.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.area_control_majority: A clearing is ruled by the faction with the most combined warriors and buildings there (birds win ties), and rule gates building slots and Vagabond aid opportunities.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Pieces move along printed paths between clearings, and you may only move if you rule the origin or destination clearing, making rule a movement lock.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.multi_use_cards: Every card can be crafted for its effect or item, spent as a supporter, slotted into the decree, or discarded for faction abilities, so each suit-marked card has many lives.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.card_as_currency: Cards are the universal currency: the Eyrie feeds them to the Decree, the Alliance stacks supporters, the Vagabond spends them to aid, and everyone discards them to craft.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_limits: In Evening each faction draws (plus bonus draws from buildings or items) and must discard down to five cards, forcing constant triage of multi-use cards.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.crafting: Crafting exhausts workshops, sympathy tokens, or roosts matching a card's suit cost to gain persistent effects, items for the Vagabond economy, or immediate victory points.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.dice_rolls: Battles roll both custom dice; the attacker deals hits equal to the higher result and defender the lower, each capped by warriors present, with extra hits against defenseless clearings.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: The two battle dice carry faces 0-3 rather than 1-6, compressing variance; the Woodland Alliance's Guerrilla Warfare ability lets it take the higher die as defender.
vector_explanations.combat.defense.ambush_cards: A defender may play an ambush card matching the clearing's suit to deal two immediate hits before the roll, cancellable only by a counter-ambush from the attacker.
vector_explanations.equipment.durability.item_wear: Vagabond items exhaust when used and are damaged by hits taken in battle; damaged items sit useless until repaired at ruins or with hammer actions.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Every turn is structured as Birdsong, Daylight, and Evening phases, but what happens in each phase is defined separately by every faction board.
vector_explanations.character.progression.victory_points: Factions score on a shared 0-30 track through their own engines: cat buildings, bird roosts, Alliance sympathy, Vagabond quests, plus universal points for crafting and removing enemy buildings.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.race_to_finish: The game ends the moment any faction reaches 30 victory points, so tempo and denial of others' scoring engines dominate the endgame.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.variable_win_conditions_: Playing a dominance card replaces your VP goal with a map condition (rule three matching clearings or two opposite corners), permanently changing your win condition mid-game.

title: Spirit Island
resolution_core: diceless card-driven powers vs a scripted invader explore/build/ravage cadence; win by fear or eliminating invaders

vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: One to four spirits defend the island together against a fully automated invader system; all information is open and victory or defeat is shared.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.staged_escalation_: The invader deck is built in three stages (I, II, III) of escalating card severity; Stage II adds the escalation effect of the chosen adversary and Stage III cards hit two land types at once.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.selectable_difficu: Adversaries (England, Brandenburg-Prussia, Sweden) and scenarios bolt onto the base rules with printed difficulty ratings, each altering setup, escalation, and loss conditions.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.scaling_difficulty: Each adversary has levels 1-6 that progressively modify the invader deck and rules, letting groups dial in difficulty from casual to brutal on a published scale.
vector_explanations.cooperation.communication.unrestricted_d: All spirit hands, energy, and board state are public and table talk is unlimited; coordinating fast/slow power timing across spirits is the intended core of play.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Every round runs Spirit phase (growth, play cards), Fast Powers, Invader phase (blighted island check, event, fear, ravage, build, explore), then Slow Powers and Time Passes.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.delayed_effects: Powers are printed Fast or Slow: fast powers resolve before the invaders act, slow powers only after ravage and build, so sequencing around the invader step is the central puzzle.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.ravage: Each invader phase, cards in the ravage slot make invaders deal damage in matching lands - 2 blight-causing damage from towns, 3 from cities - hitting the land and the Dahan simultaneously.
vector_explanations.combat.movement.dahan_counterattack: Dahan who survive a ravage strike back at 2 damage each, so powers that push invaders, gather Dahan, or grant defense convert ravages into counterattacks.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.blight_spread: Ravage damage adds blight, which destroys presence and cascades to adjacent lands when a blighted land blights again; the blight pool flipping to the Blighted Island card is a standing loss timer.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.presence_tracks: Each spirit uncovers spaces on two presence tracks (energy income and cards-per-turn) as growth adds presence to the island, tying board reach directly to engine strength.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.fear_generation: Destroying invaders and specific powers generate fear tokens; every four fear per player earns a fear card, and accumulated fear advances terror levels that ease the victory condition.
vector_explanations.character.progression.spirit_growth: The mandatory Growth step each turn chooses among options like reclaiming cards, adding presence within printed ranges, gaining power cards, or gaining energy, permanently developing the spirit.
vector_explanations.economy.production.energy: Spirits earn energy each turn equal to their uncovered energy-track value and spend it to pay power card costs, banking surplus for expensive major powers.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Played cards go to discard and only return via Reclaim growth choices, so spirits cycle small hands and must plan around reclaim tempo.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.constrained_selection_dra: Gaining a power reveals four cards from the minor or major deck; you keep exactly one and discard the rest, with majors additionally costing a forgotten card.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.success_thresh: Innate powers list element thresholds (e.g., 3 Fire, 2 Air) that trigger tiers of effect if the elements on this turn's played cards meet them, scaling with bigger card turns.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.asymmetrical_abilities: Each spirit has unique growth options, presence tracks, innate powers, special rules, and a themed starting hand - Lightning's Swift Strike plays nothing like Vital Strength of the Earth.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.forced_displacement: Push and Gather are codified keywords moving invaders, Dahan, and beasts between lands, letting spirits stage-manage where ravages and builds actually happen.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.modular_boards: The island assembles from lettered boards (A-D) with numbered lands of five terrain types, supporting different player counts and thematic or balanced layouts.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.cooperative_win_loss: All spirits win or lose together, with an optional score formula (difficulty, cards remaining, blight) to compare victories across plays.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions: The spirits lose if any spirit loses all presence, if the blight pool empties, or if the invader deck runs out before victory - three independent doom clocks.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.alternate_victory_paths: Rising terror levels relax the win condition: at Terror II you win by clearing towns and cities, at Terror III by clearing cities alone, and reaching the end of the fear deck wins outright.

title: Scythe
resolution_core: pick a different player-mat column each turn (top action + bottom action); combat via secret power dials plus cards

vector_explanations.economy.production.action_selection_grid: Each turn you place your action token on a different column of your player mat than last turn, taking its top action (Move, Trade, Produce, Bolster) and optionally its paired bottom action.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.upgrade_conversi: The Upgrade bottom action slides cubes from top-action tracks to bottom-action costs, simultaneously strengthening a top action and making a bottom action cheaper - a literal engine conversion.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resource_generation: The Produce top action generates workers, food, wood, metal, or oil on up to two (later three) of your territories, with cost escalating as more workers are deployed.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resources_on_map: Resources are not held in a supply; they physically sit on the territory that produced them and can be spent only by units on hexes you control, so armies guard their own economy.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.popularity: The popularity track (0-18) rises through Trade actions and building bonuses and falls when you attack workers or force them off hexes, functioning as a public-opinion resource.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.popularity_multiplier: End-game coins are paid per star, territory, and pair of resources at rates set by your popularity tier (3/4/5, 2/3/4, or 1/2/3), so popularity multiplies everything you did.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.asymmetrical_abilities: Five factions (Saxony, Rusviet, Polania, Nordic, Crimea) each have a unique faction ability, mech ability set, and starting position, crossed with variable player mats for further asymmetry.
vector_explanations.character.progression.stars_on_triumph_t: Players place up to six achievement stars on the Triumph Track for milestones like all upgrades, all mechs, all workers, max popularity, max power, and combat victories.
vector_explanations.logistics.movement.mech_abilities: Each deployed mech permanently unlocks a faction-specific movement or combat ability for all your units, such as Riverwalk across rivers, Underpass tunnels, or Speed +1 movement.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.terrain_movement_modifi: Rivers block movement between adjacent hexes until the right mech ability is unlocked, lakes are impassable to most units, and tunnel hexes are mutually adjacent for everyone.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.land_grab_expansion: Workers and units spread outward to control territories, since every controlled territory (Factory counts as three) pays coins at game end and hosts production and resources.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.king_of_the_hill_objectives: The central Factory hex grants its controller a powerful extra action card column and counts as three territories in final scoring, making it a persistent contested prize.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.power_dial: Combat is resolved by both sides secretly setting a power dial (0-7 power from the power track) and revealing simultaneously; the higher total wins and the loser retreats and keeps nothing.
vector_explanations.combat.defense.combat_cards: Each mech or character in the battle lets you add one secret combat card (values 2-5) to your dial total, so card holdings are hidden combat strength worth bluffing about.
vector_explanations.exploration.discovery.discovery_tiles: Encounter tokens on the map trigger encounter cards when your character reaches them, offering three choices that trade popularity and coins for resources, mechs, or workers.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.hidden_objectives: Each player holds secret objective cards (e.g., control a lake-adjacent tunnel hex set) and may complete one for a star, adding a private agenda to public development.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The instant any player places their sixth star the game ends immediately - even mid-combat - so the trigger itself is a timing weapon against slower engines.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: All scoring happens at the end: coins on hand plus popularity-tiered payouts for stars, controlled territories, and every two resources held on your hexes.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.coin: Coins are both the in-game currency for bottom-action costs and the sole victory metric, so spending money to build your engine is always spending score.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.automa_opponents: The included Automa deck runs a scripted solo opponent that moves, expands, and fights via card-driven decision rules, with multiple published difficulty levels.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.follow_actions: The Enlist bottom action recruits allies who thereafter pay you their printed bonus (coin, power, popularity, or combat card) whenever you or an adjacent opponent takes that bottom-action type.

title: Blood Rage
resolution_core: card draft per Age; diceless battles compare figure strength plus one secret battle card; most Glory after three Ages wins

vector_explanations.cards.drafting.pick_and_pass_draft: Each of the three Ages opens with a Gods' Gifts draft: players are dealt eight cards, keep one, and pass the rest, ending with six drafted cards that define their Age strategy.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Drafted hands mix battle cards, quests, upgrades, and monsters; deciding which to commit to battles versus hold as threats is the game's ongoing resource decision.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.card_counting_memory: Because every card entered play through an open draft, players can track which battle cards (like Loki's Fury or Thor's high-strength cards) remain unplayed and size up bluffs accordingly.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The game always lasts exactly three Ages, each with draft, action, discard, quest-reveal, and Ragnarok phases, ending in final scoring after the third Age.
vector_explanations.actions.points.action_point_allowance: Rage is the action currency: invading, marching, upgrading, and questing all spend Rage from a clan track that refills (and grows with Horns upgrades) at each Age start.
vector_explanations.turns.variance.pass_and_stay_out: A player who cannot or will not spend more Rage effectively drops out of the Age's action phase while others continue, so Rage efficiency controls tempo.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.dudes_on_a_map: Warriors, leaders, ships, and drafted monsters occupy the provinces ringing Yggdrasil; figure presence determines pillage strength and battle participation.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.shrinking_map: Ragnarok tokens on the Age track destroy a designated province each Age, removing it from play and granting Glory to figures sacrificed there when it falls.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.king_of_the_hill_objectives: Yggdrasil sits at the center, adjacent to every province, and several quest cards and monster abilities specifically reward presence or battles there.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.area_movement: Figures invade from the clan sheet into provinces and march between adjacent provinces, with ships occupying fjords that border two provinces at once.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.unit_recruitment: The Invade action pays Rage to place a warrior, leader, ship, or monster from your reserve into a province, with figure limits raised by Horns clan upgrades.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.military_comparison: Battles are diceless: each side totals the printed strength of its figures in the contested province plus one played battle card; the higher sum wins and losing figures die.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.battle_card_tactics: Battle cards swing fights - Thor cards add raw strength, Loki cards reward you for losing the battle, and Frigga returns your card to hand - so the card matters as much as the army.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.face_down_commitment_: All battle participants commit one card face down and reveal simultaneously; committing a worthless card to bait out an opponent's best card is core play.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: Slain figures go to Valhalla and return to your clan sheet at the Age's end, and many quests, upgrades, and Loki strategies actively reward glorious death.
vector_explanations.economy.card.engine_cards: Drafted upgrade cards attach permanently to your clan board or unit types, boosting stats (Rage, Axes, Horns) or granting abilities like monsters with battle powers.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.hidden_objectives: Quest cards are committed secretly during the action phase and revealed at Age end, scoring Glory and stat boosts only if their condition (e.g., dominate a specific province) holds.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.immediate_incremental_sco: Glory is awarded on the spot for winning battles, pillaging provinces, and sacrificing figures to Ragnarok, advancing each clan's marker around the board edge.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: After the third Age, final Glory is added for fulfilled quests and stat-track bonuses from Axes, Horns, and Rage positions, deciding the winner.
vector_explanations.character.progression.victory_points: Glory is the single victory metric, tracked around the board's edge; every mechanism, from pillaging to dying well, is priced in Glory.

title: Nemesis
resolution_core: action cards spent for actions and costs; noise rolls draw intruders; semi-coop with secret objectives and an intruder token bag

vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.semi_coop_selfish_: Crew members share the problem of surviving the Nemesis but win individually by completing personal objectives, so cooperation is constant, conditional, and revocable.
vector_explanations.politics.intrigue.secret_objectives: Each player holds two secret objective cards (corporate and personal) and must discard one when the first intruder appears, keeping goals like 'send the signal' or 'kill another character'.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.variable_win_conditions_: Victory is checked per player: you win only if you fulfilled your kept objective and survived (in hibernation, an escape pod, or the intact ship), regardless of others' fates.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.sanctioned_lying_scop: The rules explicitly allow players to say anything about their objectives and intentions - lying about whether you need the engines sabotaged is sanctioned play.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.asymmetric_team_ro: Six characters (Captain, Pilot, Scout, Mechanic, Scientist, Soldier) each have a unique ten-card action deck and starting gear, giving every crew member distinct capabilities.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.action_cards_hand: On your turn you take two actions, each paid by playing or discarding cards from your character's action deck, so your options are whatever your current hand allows.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.card_as_currency: Action costs are paid by discarding other cards from hand, and basic actions like movement also burn cards, making every card both an ability and a currency.
vector_explanations.stealth.detection.noise_rolls: Moving in unexplored or intruder-free areas forces a noise roll; results place noise markers in numbered corridors around your room, and a 'danger' face triggers an encounter directly.
vector_explanations.exploration.procedures.noise_attraction: If a noise roll would place a marker in a corridor that already has one, an encounter occurs instead: an intruder spawns in your room, punishing repeated movement through loud zones.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.bag_building_draws: Intruder encounters and event effects draw tokens from the intruder bag, whose mix of larvae, creepers, adults, breeders, and the queen evolves during play - the blank token even adds an adult to the bag.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.enemy_spawn_rules: Encounter draws consult the intruder board: the token drawn plus the number of characters present set the spawned intruder's type and whether it attacks immediately (surprise).
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.event_deck_pressur: Every round's event phase advances the time track, moves intruders toward noise, and resolves an event card that shifts fires, malfunctions, or intruder behavior.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.tile_reveal: The ship's rooms start face down; entering one reveals which room it is and places an item counter, so even the geography of the Nemesis must be scouted under threat.
vector_explanations.exploration.site.room_searching: Each room's item counter allows Search actions drawing from the red (military), green (technical), or blue (medical) item decks depending on room color, until the counter is exhausted.
vector_explanations.equipment.economy_of_gear.loot_decks: Items come from three color-coded loot decks plus crafted combinations, and heavy gear like the flamethrower carries limited ammo tracked on the card.
vector_explanations.equipment.crafting.recipe_crafting: The Craft action combines two specific item cards (listed on the crafted item) into gear like the makeshift taser or molotov, letting junk finds become weapons.
vector_explanations.logistics.downtime.laboratory_research: In the Laboratory, characters can analyze an intruder carcass or egg to reveal one of the hidden intruder weakness tokens, unlocking bonuses against the brood for everyone.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.contamination_cards: Intruder attacks and slime shuffle contamination cards into your action deck; they are dead cards when drawn, and at game end they are scanned - a failed scan on an infected card kills you.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.wound_cards: Damage beyond light wounds inflicts serious wound cards (broken arm, haemorrhage) with permanent penalties; a character with three serious wounds who takes another dies.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: Combat and noise use custom dice: the shooting and close-combat dice mix hits, misses, and special faces, while the noise die's numbered faces map to specific corridor exits.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.fire_malfunctions: Fire markers damage characters and rooms (a room with fire can burn out), and malfunction markers disable room functions until repaired, both spread by event cards.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Characters and intruders move room to room along corridor connections printed on the modular ship board, with door tokens able to seal individual corridors.
vector_explanations.spatial.geometry.chokepoints: Closing doors blocks intruder movement and noise placement through that corridor, letting players funnel the brood - until an intruder destroys the door from its side.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Each round runs a player phase (alternating two-action turns until all pass) followed by an event phase: time track, intruder attacks, fire damage, and an event card in fixed order.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The game ends when the self-destruct countdown finishes, the ship jumps to its destination coordinates, or every character is dead, hibernated, or escaped - whichever comes first.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions: Even objective-holders lose if the ship's engines are misconfigured or destroyed, if the wrong destination is locked in, or if their character dies or fails the contamination scan.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.elimination: Character death is permanent player elimination (bar the official variant), and death can come silently from a contamination scan revealed only at the very end.

title: Eldritch Horror
resolution_core: skill tests roll d6 pools equal to skill, 5-6 succeed; solve three Mysteries before the Doom track or Ancient One awakening ends the world

vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.fully_cooperative: One to eight investigators cooperate globally against a single Ancient One; there are no hidden traitors and the team wins or loses as one.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.doom_track_escalat: The Doom track ticks down from the Ancient One's starting value; gates opening and the Omen track advancing over matching gate colors accelerate it, and Doom 0 awakens the Ancient One.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.event_deck_pressur: Every round ends with a Mythos card whose icons spawn gates and monsters, trigger Reckoning effects, spread Rumors, and drain Sanity, forming the game's relentless metronome.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.enemy_spawn_rules: Gate spawns are drawn from a seeded gate stack tied to city spaces, each bringing a monster from the cup; spreading gates left unclosed feed Doom advancement through the Omen system.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.selectable_difficu: Choosing the Ancient One (Azathoth, Cthulhu, Shub-Niggurath, Yog-Sothoth) swaps in its own Mystery deck, Research deck, and special rules, effectively selecting scenario and difficulty.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.dice_pool_succe: Every test rolls d6s equal to the tested skill (Lore, Influence, Observation, Strength, Will) plus modifiers; each 5 or 6 is a success, and most tests need just one.
vector_explanations.luck.mitigation.dice_rerolls: Spending a Clue token rerolls all dice that failed on a test, and assets like Whiskey or conditions like Blessed (successes on 4+) further bend the odds.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.clues: Clue tokens spawn on the map via Mythos cards and are gathered through Research encounters keyed to the Ancient One, doubling as reroll fuel and Mystery-solving currency.
vector_explanations.mental.sanity.sanity_meter: Each investigator tracks Sanity separately from Health; horror damage from encounters and monsters reduces it, and reaching zero devours or maddens the investigator.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Health tracks physical damage from combat and events; at zero the investigator is defeated, leaving a token on the map for teammates to encounter.
vector_explanations.combat.status.conditions_tracking: Condition cards like Detained, Injured, Cursed, Dark Pact, and Debt attach to investigators with hidden reverse sides that Reckoning effects flip for nasty surprises.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.dice_pools: Fighting a monster resolves a Will test against its horror rating, then a Strength test against its toughness, with successes reducing damage taken and wounding the monster.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.skill_roll_casting: Spell cards are cast with Lore tests; the number of successes determines the effect read from the spell's hidden back side, which the caster cannot preview.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.backlash_damage: Spell backs frequently punish weak castings with Sanity loss, Conditions, or worse - the flip side of Flesh Ward or Shriveling can maim its own caster.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.artifact_rules: Artifacts are a separate, more powerful item deck (Ruby of R'lyeh, silver key relics) usually gained from Expedition encounters and immune to normal loss effects that strip possessions.
vector_explanations.economy.market.loan_taking: The Bank Loan mechanic grants two extra dice worth of purchasing via a Debt condition; its hidden back can later demand repayment or send collectors after you at Reckoning.
vector_explanations.downtime.activities.shopping_resupply: The Acquire Assets action is an Influence test whose successes buy assets from a four-card reserve row, abstracting shopping into a skill-driven market.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Investigators move along printed paths on a world map of named cities, seas, and wilderness spaces, buying ship and rail tickets to extend a move along matching routes.
vector_explanations.exploration.discovery.rumor_tables: Rumor Mythos cards land on specific spaces with their own countdown clocks and solve conditions; ignoring one long enough triggers a global penalty for the rest of the game.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Rounds run Action phase (two actions each: travel, rest, trade, acquire, prepare), Encounter phase (location, monster, or gate encounters), then Mythos phase, in fixed order.
vector_explanations.campaign.continuity.replacement_characte: A defeated investigator is replaced by a new one next turn; the old character's body or maddened self stays on the map as an encounter that can recover their possessions and clues.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Victory requires solving three Mystery cards specific to the Ancient One (e.g., banishing Cthulhu's Star Spawn), each an explicit objective with published requirements.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions: The team loses if the Doom track hits zero and the awakened Ancient One's final Mystery fails, if the Mythos deck runs out, or if all investigators are eliminated.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.cooperative_win_loss: All investigators share one collective outcome: banish the Ancient One together or watch the world end together - there is no individual scoring.

title: Dead of Winter: A Crossroads Game
resolution_core: action dice pool per turn; semi-coop main objective plus secret objectives, possible betrayer, and triggered Crossroads cards

vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.semi_coop_selfish_: The colony shares a main objective, but every player also holds a secret objective and only wins if their personal condition is met when the main objective resolves.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.traitor_mechanics: The secret objective deck can seed a Betrayer whose card demands the colony fail plus a personal condition, so every stray action invites suspicion.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.variable_win_conditions_: Win checks are individual: colonists win only if the main objective succeeds AND their secret objective is done; an exiled Betrayer wins on a separate exile card's terms.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Each game uses one main objective card (e.g., gather samples, build barricades, survive waves) that defines the round count and the colony's explicit success condition.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.multiple_loss_conditions: Everyone (except a successful Betrayer) loses immediately if morale hits zero or the round track expires before the main objective completes.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.doom_track_escalat: Morale is a shared doom track lowered by deaths, waste pile overflow, and crises; each survivor death also directly drops morale, tightening the spiral.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.random_plot_twists: Each turn the player to your right draws a Crossroads card and silently watches for its trigger; if you spring it, a narrative choice with branching mechanical consequences is read aloud.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.dice_action_pools: At turn start you roll action dice (survivors plus one); each die is assigned to attack, search, barricade, clean waste, or attract zombies, with die-value minimums for attacks and searches.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: The twelve-sided exposure die is rolled for attacks and risky moves: most faces are safe, but frostbite and wound faces injure, and the tooth symbol kills the survivor outright.
vector_explanations.cooperation.assistance.shared_resources: Crisis cards demand the colony collectively contribute matching item cards face down each round; shortfalls trigger the crisis penalty against everyone.
vector_explanations.deduction.bluffing.face_down_commitment_: Because crisis contributions are secret until revealed, a Betrayer can slip in wrong-type cards, each of which cancels a valid contribution and sabotages the crisis.
vector_explanations.politics.governance.council_votes: Any player may call an exile vote; a majority strips the accused of colony membership, and the exiled player draws a new exile objective, betraying or vindicating suspicions.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.asymmetric_team_ro: Players control multiple unique survivors (Sparky the stunt dog, Forest Plum the mall Santa), each with an ability, influence value, and attack/search die requirements.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.enemy_spawn_rules: Zombies spawn every round at the colony and each occupied location based on survivor and helpless-survivor counts, filling numbered entry spaces in order.
vector_explanations.exploration.procedures.noise_attraction: Loud actions place noise tokens at your location, and each noise token forces a die roll that can spawn extra zombies there during the colony phase.
vector_explanations.equipment.economy_of_gear.loot_decks: Each location (Grocery Store, Police Station, Hospital, School, Library, Gas Station) has its own themed item deck searched with a die of sufficient value.
vector_explanations.luck.push.escalating_risk: After a search you may keep digging: each extra card drawn from the location deck costs one noise token, trading zombie pressure for better loot.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.food_tokens: The colony must pay food tokens equal to survivor count thresholds every round; missing the payment adds starvation tokens that drop morale each round they persist.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.wound_track: Survivors track wounds and frostbite tokens; a third wound kills, and frostbite adds a new wound every turn until treated with medicine at the hospital.
vector_explanations.combat.defense.fortress_siege: Barricade tokens placed at entry spaces absorb arriving zombies one for one; once entrances overflow, each excess zombie kills a survivor at that location.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Rounds alternate player turns (dice, movement, actions, Crossroads triggers) with a colony phase that pays food, checks waste, resolves the crisis, spawns zombies, and checks objectives.

title: Betrayal at House on the Hill
resolution_core: trait rolls with custom 0/1/2 d6s vs thresholds; omen draws force haunt rolls until one of 50 haunts begins as one-vs-many

vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.tile_reveal: The house is built during play: stepping through an unexplored doorway draws a room tile from the stack and places it, so every game maps a different mansion.
vector_explanations.exploration.discovery.first_visit_trigge: Room symbols force a draw (event, omen, or item) on first entry, and rooms like the Mystic Elevator, Coal Chute, and Collapsed Room have printed movement or damage effects.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.terrain_placement_rest: Each room tile is marked for ground floor, upper floor, and/or basement; a drawn tile that cannot legally go on your floor is set aside and you draw again.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Explorers move up to their current Speed in connected rooms through doorways, with barriers like the Vault or locked passages altering connectivity.
vector_explanations.cards.deck_manipulation.multiple_deck_so: Three separate decks (events, items, omens) are drawn from based on the symbol of the room just revealed, giving exploration three distinct risk-reward flavors.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.coop_competitive_p: The first act is cooperative exploration; the moment the haunt begins the game becomes traitor versus heroes with entirely new rules, goals, and often new monsters.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.one_vs_many: After the haunt starts, one traitor (usually) plays against all surviving heroes, taking monster turns and following a private script from the Traitor's Tome.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.staged_escalation_: Every omen drawn forces a haunt roll of six dice against the total number of omens in play; failure flips the game into the haunt act, so tension ratchets with each omen.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.traitor_mechanics: One explorer is revealed as the traitor when the haunt begins, determined by a chart crossing the triggering omen with the room where it was drawn.
vector_explanations.deduction.hidden_roles.role_reveal_timin: The traitor's identity is unknown during exploration and revealed only by the haunt chart lookup, sometimes to that player's own surprise (and some haunts have hidden or no traitors).
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.asymmetric_informatio: The traitor leaves the room to read the Traitor's Tome while heroes read Secrets of Survival aloud; each side knows only its own win condition and hidden rules.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Each of the 50 numbered haunts defines explicit victory conditions - escape the house, perform a ritual, destroy the monster - checked exactly as written.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.variable_win_conditions_: Traitor and heroes pursue different, often mutually exclusive goals within the same haunt, and some haunts give individual survivors separate escape conditions.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: All rolls use special d6s with faces 0, 0, 1, 1, 2, 2, so an N-dice roll ranges from 0 to 2N with a strong central hump.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.success_thresh: Trait rolls total dice equal to the current Speed, Might, Sanity, or Knowledge value against a printed threshold set by the event, room, or haunt instruction.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.opposed_rolls: Combat is an opposed Might roll (or another trait with certain weapons/haunts); the higher total deals damage equal to the difference to the loser.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.wound_track: Physical damage slides Speed and Might down their printed number ladders; mental damage slides Sanity and Knowledge, and each explorer's card has unique ladder values.
vector_explanations.mental.sanity.sanity_meter: Sanity is one of four sliding trait tracks; mental attacks and terrifying events push it toward the skull, crippling trait rolls that depend on it.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.consumable_items: Item cards like Dynamite, the Angel Feather, and Healing Salve are carried, traded in the same room, dropped, and often expended for one-shot effects.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.enemy_spawn_rules: Haunt scenarios spawn monsters with their own turns, movement, and attack rules run by the traitor, from zombie hordes to a single stalking beast.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.elimination: When any trait reaches the skull symbol the explorer dies; during a haunt dead heroes are out of the game (and in some haunts return as the traitor's minions).

title: Star Wars: Rebellion
resolution_core: leaders dispatched to secret missions vs opposed skill rolls; asymmetric duel - Empire hunts the hidden base, Rebels run out the reputation clock

vector_explanations.politics.factions.asymmetrical_abilities: The Empire fields Star Destroyers, the Death Star, and overwhelming production; the Rebellion fields cheap troops, guerrilla missions, and objective cards - the two sides share almost no rules of thumb.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.variable_win_conditions_: The Empire wins only by finding and destroying the hidden Rebel base; the Rebellion wins only by raising its reputation until the reputation marker meets the advancing time marker.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.sudden_death_win_conditi: Destroying the Rebel base (with the time marker yet to meet reputation) ends the game instantly for the Empire, as does the marker collision for the Rebels.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The time track advances every round while Rebel objectives pull the reputation marker backward toward it, so the whole game is a closing scissors of two clocks.
vector_explanations.stealth.pursuit.hidden_movement: The Rebel base is a face-down probe card set aside at setup; Rebel units can even sit on the base space invisibly, and the Rapid Mobilization mission relocates the base secretly.
vector_explanations.deduction.inference.negative_inference: The Empire draws probe droid cards naming systems where the base is NOT, and narrows the location by crossing off possibilities while watching Rebel behavior for tells.
vector_explanations.actions.programming.hidden_orders: During assignment both players place leaders face down on mission cards; missions are revealed and resolved one at a time in the command phase, inviting feints and counter-placements.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_placement: Leaders are the action economy: each can be spent on one mission or one system activation per round, and recruiting new leaders expands your action pool.
vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.opposed_rolls: A revealed mission rolls dice equal to the assigned leaders' matching skill icons versus dice from any opposing leader sent to block; successes versus successes decide it.
vector_explanations.politics.intrigue.espionage: Mission cards cover sabotage (production-blocking markers), intelligence gathering, carbonite freezing, and Death Star plans theft, all resolved through the leader-versus-leader system.
vector_explanations.cooperation.assistance.rescue_mechanics: Captured leaders (including frozen-in-carbonite heroes) can be freed by dedicated rescue missions, while the Empire can interrogate captives to accelerate the probe deck.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.hidden_objectives: The Rebel player builds a deck of secret objective cards (destroy a Star Destroyer, liberate a subjugated system) whose completion drives reputation toward victory.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.influence_placement: System loyalty markers shift through diplomacy missions and subjugation; loyal systems feed each side's production wheel while sabotage and subjugation suppress it.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.unit_recruitment: During refresh, loyal and subjugated systems' build icons queue units matching their printed types - shipyards make capital ships, populous worlds make troopers.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.delayed_effects: Built units enter a numbered build queue and slide one slot each refresh phase, arriving on the map only after their production delay elapses.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: Combat rolls red and black custom dice whose faces mix solid hits, crits, and lightsaber icons for tactic-card triggers, with unit type dictating dice color and count.
vector_explanations.combat.defense.combat_cards: Each combat round both sides draw tactic cards up to their best leader's tactic values (space and ground separately), spending them to block hits or add effects.
vector_explanations.combat.space.battle_resolution: Battles resolve in two theaters - space and ground - simultaneously in the same system, with each theater's units, dice, and tactic decks kept separate.
vector_explanations.vehicles.operation.cargo_capacity: Ground units cannot cross space on their own: transports and capital ships have printed capacity, so invasions are constrained by lift as much as by firepower.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Activating a system with a leader moves ships from adjacent systems into it along the printed galaxy adjacencies, with leaders required to lead any attack move.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Each round runs Assignment (secret leader placement), Command (alternating mission reveals and system activations), and Refresh (retrieve leaders, slide build queues, advance time).

title: War of the Ring (Second Edition)
resolution_core: action dice pools with symbol faces drive all play; battles roll d6s hitting on 5+; Ring corruption race vs military victory points

vector_explanations.actions.selection.dice_action_pools: Each turn the Free Peoples roll four action dice and the Shadow up to ten; every die face is an action type (Army, Character, Muster, Event, Will of the West), and each die spent is one action.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.custom_dice_faces: The custom action dice differ by side - the Shadow die carries the Eye face that feeds the Hunt, while only the Free Peoples die shows Will of the West - so the randomizer itself is asymmetric.
vector_explanations.turns.order.alternating_unit_activation: Players alternate resolving one action die at a time rather than taking whole turns, so the Shadow's larger pool grants extra actions at the string's end.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Rounds follow a fixed sequence: recover dice and draw cards, Fellowship declaration, Shadow Hunt allocation, action dice rolls, alternating action resolution, and military victory check.
vector_explanations.politics.diplomacy.political_track: Each Free Peoples nation sits on a political track and must be advanced (by war events, attacks, or Muster dice) through passive steps to the At War position before fully mobilizing.
vector_explanations.politics.warfare_politics.war_declaratio: Only nations At War may attack, move into other nations, and muster freely; Shadow aggression can drag reluctant nations like Gondor and Rohan toward war for you.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.asymmetrical_abilities: The Shadow wields endless mustering, the Hunt, and Nazgul; the Free Peoples rely on scarce armies, unique companions, and the desperate secrecy of the Ring-bearers - two different games in one box.
vector_explanations.stealth.detection.hunt_for_ring: Each Fellowship move triggers a Hunt roll of d6s equal to Eye dice allocated (successes on 6, +1 per prior move this turn); a success draws a Hunt tile dealing corruption or revealing the Fellowship.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.bag_building_draws: Hunt damage comes from a pool of chit tiles drawn blind, mixing numbered corruption, the Eye, and special tiles; certain events seed extra tiles into the pool as the game progresses.
vector_explanations.stealth.pursuit.hidden_movement: The Fellowship advances on a hidden step track rather than the map; the Shadow only learns its true region when the Hunt reveals it or the Ring-bearers declare themselves.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.corruption_track: Frodo's corruption track runs 0-12: Hunt tiles and event cards add corruption, companions can be sacrificed as guides to absorb it, and 12 corruption is an immediate Shadow victory.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.multi_use_cards: Every event card has two halves - a strategy/event effect and a combat card effect - and playing it one way forfeits the other, doubling every hand decision.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.dice_rolls: Battles roll up to five d6s per round (capped by combat strength), hitting on 5+; leadership values grant reroll dice, and rounds repeat until retreat or annihilation.
vector_explanations.luck.mitigation.dice_rerolls: Leadership from named characters and Nazgul converts missed combat dice into rerolls, making leaders force multipliers rather than just extra units.
vector_explanations.luck.mitigation.resource_to_modify_dice: The three Elven Rings, passed from Free Peoples to Shadow as they are used, each let their holder change one action die result once per turn, bending the whole action economy.
vector_explanations.combat.defense.combat_cards: Each battle round both sides may secretly choose a combat card from hand, revealed simultaneously to modify that round - forfeiting a strategic event to win a tactical exchange.
vector_explanations.combat.defense.fortress_siege: Armies retreating into strongholds force sieges where attackers hit only on 6s and defenders on 5s; sieges grind on in successive turns until reduced or relieved.
vector_explanations.combat.scale.mass_combat: Combat is fought between armies of regular and elite units (max ten per region) representing whole hosts, with elites able to absorb two hits by downgrading.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.dudes_on_a_map: Plastic armies, leaders, and minions occupy the named regions of Middle-earth; control of victory-point cities and strongholds is marked and scored continuously.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.area_movement: Army dice move armies between adjacent regions (two separate armies per Army die), with sea crossings, mountains, and enemy strongholds constraining lines of advance.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.unit_recruitment: Muster dice place new regulars, elites, and leaders in free settlements of At War nations, with the Shadow's dead units returning to its reinforcement pool while Free Peoples losses are permanent.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.alternate_victory_paths: The Shadow wins militarily at 10 VP of captured settlements or by corrupting Frodo; the Free Peoples win at 4 VP of Shadow strongholds or by destroying the Ring - four distinct victory doors.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.sudden_death_win_conditi: The Ring reaching Mount Doom's Crack of Doom or corruption hitting 12 ends the game on the spot, regardless of the military situation anywhere else on the map.

title: Mage Knight Board Game
resolution_core: deckbuilt hand of multi-use cards powered by mana from source dice; phased combat (ranged/siege, block, damage, attack) with no dice in battle

vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.starter_deck_upgrades: Each hero starts with a personal 16-card deed deck and adds Advanced Actions, Spells, and Artifacts earned from leveling, monasteries, and conquests, tuning the deck across the game.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.multi_use_cards: Every deed card has a basic effect and a stronger mana-powered effect, and any card can be played sideways as a generic Move 1, Attack 1, Block 1, or Influence 1.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_limits: Your hand limit starts at five and grows via level-ups and conquered keeps or cities near your position, directly converting territorial success into bigger turns.
vector_explanations.simulation.magic.mana_source: The Source is a shared pool of rolled mana dice; each player may use one Source die per turn, taking its color to power a card and rerolling it back at turn end.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.mana_dice: Source dice showing gold act as wild mana by day but are unusable at night, while black dice fuel powerful effects only at night, so the dice pool itself obeys the day/night law.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.mana_points: Mana crystals stored in your inventory (capped at three per color) are spent as guaranteed mana, complementing the gamble of the shared Source dice.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.day_night_cycle: Rounds alternate day and night: night raises movement costs in forests, lowers them in deserts, hides rampaging enemy tokens, and flips which wild mana is legal.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.tile_reveal: The countryside and core map tiles are revealed by Explore moves as heroes push outward, so the map itself is a resource paid for with movement points.
vector_explanations.exploration.site.keyed_locations: Every site type has fixed printed rules - keeps and mage towers demand assaults, dungeons and tombs are night-rules delves, villages allow recruiting and pillaging, monasteries sell advanced actions.
vector_explanations.exploration.discovery.fog_of_war: Garrisons of keeps, towers, and cities stay face down until you are adjacent (or assault), so attacking sight-unseen is a codified gamble on hidden enemy tokens.
vector_explanations.logistics.movement.terrain_cost: Each terrain has a numbered move cost that day/night and skills modify; lakes and mountains are impassable, and moving is the same currency used to explore new tiles.
vector_explanations.combat.defense.block_phase: Combat is strictly phased: ranged/siege attacks first, then blocks against each enemy attack (halved if the attack element resists), then unblocked damage assignment, then your attack phase.
vector_explanations.combat.ranged.siege_phase: Fortified sites ignore ordinary ranged attacks in the opening phase - only Siege attacks touch them - and doubly fortified cities demand siege even from within.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.wound_cards: Damage deals wound cards straight into your hand (or units), clogging future turns; too many wounds at once knocks your hero out, and healing must purge them.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.unit_recruitment: Influence points recruit units - peasants in villages, monks in monasteries, elite regiments in keeps and cities - each with a recruitment cost and location requirement.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.reputation_track: The reputation track slides from +5 to -7 as you liberate sites or burn monasteries; it modifies all Influence interactions, and at rock bottom villagers refuse to deal with you at all.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.exhaust_and_refresh_ac: Recruited units are spent (turned sideways) when used for attack, block, or abilities and only ready again at the round's start, so each unit is roughly one action per round.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: Fame from every enemy killed and site conquered advances a track; alternating levels grant either a skill token plus Advanced Action or a command token and larger hand/armor.
vector_explanations.character.progression.perks: Skill tokens drawn on level-up are unique per hero (Norowas's leadership, Tovak's cold resistance) and give repeatable once-per-turn or once-per-round powers.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.artifact_rules: Artifacts from dungeons and tombs are the deck's power ceiling: each can be played for a strong ongoing effect or destroyed (banner artifacts attach to units) for a one-shot ultimate.
vector_explanations.turns.order.card_determined_initiative: At each round start players simultaneously pick numbered day/night Tactic cards; the chosen number sets turn order and grants that tactic's unique benefit.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: Scenarios run a fixed number of alternating day and night rounds (six in Full Conquest), and a player emptying their deed deck can announce the End of Round to cut everyone else short.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.solo_mode_design: Solo rules ship in the box with a dummy player whose deck consumes tactics and reveals map tiles, and scenario variants support full co-op assaults on cities.
vector_explanations.scoring.victory.scenario_objective_victo: Scenarios define the goal - typically conquering a set number of cities before the final round - with printed setup, round counts, and scoring adjustments per scenario.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Final scoring adds Fame to achievement bonuses (Greatest Knowledge, Greatest Loot, city conquest titles) with penalties for wounds, crowning the highest total.

title: Arkham Horror: The Card Game
resolution_core: skill value plus committed card icons plus a chaos-bag token vs test difficulty; campaign scenarios advance act/agenda decks

vector_explanations.simulation.environment.chaos_bag: Every skill test pulls a token from the chaos bag - numeric modifiers, investigator-specific elder signs, and scenario-defined skulls, cultists, and the auto-fail tentacle - instead of rolling dice.
vector_explanations.cooperation.challenge.selectable_difficu: Easy, Standard, Hard, and Expert each define a different token mix for the chaos bag, and scenario reference cards redefine what skull or cultist tokens do per scenario.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.variable_tn: Tests compare modified skill against printed difficulties: a location's shroud for investigating, an enemy's fight or evade value, or a treachery card's stated test number.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.asymmetric_starting_d: Each investigator builds a deck under personal deckbuilding rules (class access, level ranges, card pool sizes) plus mandatory signature cards unique to that investigator.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.card_crafting_upgrade: Between scenarios, experience buys higher-level versions of cards or new cards into the deck, evolving each investigator's toolkit across the campaign.
